curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: equation of a straight line (y = mx + c)
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Understand and use the form y = mx + c to describe straight lines, interpret m as gradient and c as the y-intercept, and explore how changing these parameters affects graphs and real-world models such as income growth. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

verse_ality.driving_question: How can the equation y = mx + c help us model change, make predictions, and reason about fairness in real-world systems?

knowing_world.focus: Straight lines can be represented by y = mx + c, where m controls steepness (gradient) and c controls where the line crosses the y-axis (y-intercept). Students test predictions by adjusting sliders and observing the graph.
key_concepts: Linear equation form y = mx + c
Effect of changing m on steepness and direction
Effect of changing c on vertical shift
Predicting graph changes from parameter changes
tools: Canva embed: interactive sliders for m and c to explore how the line changes
activities: Adjust m and c and predict the effect before testing it.
Describe how the graph changes when only m changes.
Describe how the graph changes when only c changes.

human_story.focus: Linear equations are used as models of real-world systems. Students compare two income-growth models using y = mx + c, discussing which helps families reach above-poverty income sooner and what feels realistic or ethical.
justice_prompts: Which model gets families above poverty faster?
Which feels more realistic or ethical?
tools: Canva embed: Poverty Modelling Tool for comparing linear income models
activities: Compare a training programme model (gradual rise) with a cash investment model (higher starting value).
Use country income context links (e.g., GNI per capita) to discuss how models relate to reality.

expressive_self.focus: Students express what a line could represent in their own life, using sketches, writing prompts, or voice notes to connect mathematical structure to meaning.
activities: Sketch a line and describe what it could mean in your life.
Write: “If I were a line, I would…”
Record a voice note describing the journey of a line.

designed_world.focus: Students design and compare lines by controlling slope and intercept, then generalise how families of lines behave and how equations can be constructed to meet constraints like intersecting at a given point.
design_challenges: Graph two lines with the same slope but different y-intercepts.
Graph two lines with the same y-intercept but different slopes.
Write equations for lines that intersect at (2, 5) and explain how slopes and intercepts change.

regulation_supports: Breathe in for a rising slope and out for a falling slope.
Draw a line in the air and notice the pace, steepness, and ease.

pathways.name: Final Task - Mastering Straight Lines
pathways.focus: Create and compare two straight-line graphs to demonstrate understanding of how slope and intercept affect a line.
products: A graph showing two lines with explanations
One example: same slope, different y-intercepts (labelled)
One example: same y-intercept, different slopes (labelled)
A short explanation of what you learned from the differences

required: Plot coordinates on a Cartesian grid
Understand gradient as a measure of steepness
Recognise the y-axis intercept as the point where a graph crosses the y-axis
Work confidently with positive and negative numbers

builds_toward: Finding gradient and intercept from graphs
Writing equations of lines from points or contexts
Interpreting linear models (rates and starting values)
